quote: No. I just I thought about it because I was always thinking about getting solar, but I was always in an apartment. So Yeah. You know, this is the first time I've owned a house.
stewart_read: Customer reveals a long-held aspiration for solar — this isn't casual curiosity, it's deferred intent finally becoming actionable. The codex flags interest_in_solar as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and calls for 60+ seconds of depth. Rep responded with a brief validation and moved immediately to the credit qualifier. The emotional weight of 'I always wanted this but couldn't' was available to anchor the entire pitch and was left untouched.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been moving efficiently through verify to protect call time — but the codex explicitly grades depth here, and this customer was offering it freely.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex note on interest_in_solar depth grading. The customer handed them the emotional anchor of the call in the first 90 seconds. A suggested rephrase direction: reflect the aspiration back ('So this is the first time you've actually been able to act on it — what made you finally pull the trigger?') before moving to credit. This is the moment that separates a booked appointment from a booked appointment the customer is excited about.

quote: For any of the programs or incentives going on right now, you just need to have a credit score above 670. I'm assuming that's the case for you?
stewart_read: Rep frames the credit qualifier as an assumption ('I'm assuming that's the case for you?') rather than a direct ask. Customer confirms with 'Yeah. Yeah.' — the qualifier is technically captured. However, the assumption framing skips the script's template entirely and may be papering over a real qualification check. Per the codex's unresolved policy question, it's unclear whether setter DQ on stated credit is even the right procedure — but the assumption framing means the rep didn't actually ask, they led the witness.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may be using assumption framing deliberately to reduce friction on a warm lead — a reasonable social move. But the codex flags this as a hard qualifier, and assumption framing on a hard qualifier is a procedural gap regardless of outcome.
coaching_implication: Flag the assumption framing. The direct ask ('Is that the case for you?') is low-friction on a warm lead and doesn't require the leading setup. More importantly, surface the unresolved codex policy question (A6) with the manager: if the policy is 'let the closer's credit pull decide,' the entire qualifier framing changes and this becomes a non-issue.

quote: I think the average that I've seen is between 1,100 and 1,400 around that range per month because I do have an EV as well.
stewart_read: Customer volunteers an EV — a high-load signal that makes this lead materially more valuable. The 1,100–1,400 kWh range is already above average; the EV explains why and signals the load will stay elevated. Rep acknowledged the EV ('Okay. You got an EV. That's good to know') but immediately pivoted to a personal EV conversation rather than anchoring the load implication to bill pain. The bill anchor was captured as data and dropped — the codex's 'capture + flip' pattern was not executed.
alternative_hypothesis: The EV tangent could be read as deliberate rapport-building before the booking ask. But the tangent ran ~90 seconds and produced no qualification value, and the bill was never emotionally flipped at any point in the call.
coaching_implication: Two moves were available here that the rep missed: (1) anchor the EV load to the bill pain ('So your bill is already high and it's going to stay that way as long as you're charging at home — that's exactly the situation where solar math works best'); (2) note the EV for the closer as a load-sizing input. Neither happened. Show the rep the codex's bill_anchoring 'capture + flip' pattern and use this moment as the example.

quote: Alright. And do you like your EV? I've been thinking of getting one, but I just I don't know.
stewart_read: Rep pivots from a high-value qualification signal (EV load) to a personal question about their own car-buying deliberation. This is not rapport-building in service of the call — it's scope drift that burns ~90 seconds and pulls the customer into an advisory role. The customer obliges with a detailed Tesla recommendation (04:37–05:20), which is warm but off-track. The call recovers, but the bill anchor window closes without a flip.
alternative_hypothesis: The EV tangent kept the customer engaged and may have contributed to the easy booking close. Possible — but the same warmth was available without the 90-second detour, and the bill anchor was the casualty.
coaching_implication: This is a coachable habit, not a one-off. The rep's personal EV question is the kind of move that feels like rapport but is actually scope drift. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'What were you trying to accomplish with that question, and did it move the call forward?' The goal is self-awareness, not criticism — the rep is clearly likable, and that's an asset. The coaching is about directing that energy toward the bill anchor rather than away from it.

quote: I did, you know, go through I someone from reached out, and I talked to him as well. I got a price there as well. I just wanted to see what is it similar? Is it different? I mean, they had a fifteen year plan.
stewart_read: First touch of the Sunrun comparison. Rep executed the correct strategic direction — validating comparison shopping aligns with the codex's already_saw_design comparison_framing rebuttal. But the second half of the move ('our equipment and price points can be different') was omitted. This is a partial execution gap: the rep got the strategy right and left the curiosity hook out. Not a reactive failure, but an incomplete one.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been saving differentiation for the specialist — but the codex's rebuttal is designed for the setter stage, and the customer was explicitly asking 'is it similar? is it different?' A direct answer was invited.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the full already_saw_design rebuttal template. The validation half is there; the differentiation hook is missing. In the 1-on-1, role-play the second half: after 'getting multiple quotes is a good idea,' add 'we use different equipment than most companies and our price points can vary — that's exactly why it's worth a look.' Low lift, high value.

quote: I got a quote from Sunrun roughly around $1.60 something a month. And so I thought that wasn't bad with the with the battery with two batteries.
stewart_read: Second touch of the Sunrun comparison — now with a concrete price anchor ($160/month, two batteries). Rep said nothing in response. No comparison framing, no curiosity creation, no differentiation. The customer handed the rep the most actionable competitive data point of the call and received silence before the rep moved to button-up. This is the compounding miss: the first touch was partial execution; the second touch was no execution.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been prioritizing the booking close over competitive engagement at this late stage — a reasonable sequencing instinct. But the customer raised the Sunrun number in the context of evaluating Ion, not as a closing objection. A brief comparison hook here would have strengthened the appointment, not delayed it.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 01:41 touch in the 1-on-1 to show the pattern. The customer raised Sunrun twice; the rep engaged neither time at full depth. The closer now walks in with $160/month + two batteries as the unchallenged benchmark. A suggested rephrase direction for the second touch: 'That's actually a useful number to have — our specialist will build you a design with real numbers so you can put them side by side. That's the only way to know if you're getting the better deal.' No fabricated numbers, creates curiosity, advances the appointment.

quote: K. Yeah. And we have batteries as well. I know we have, we have the Tesla Powerwall batteries, and then we also have another one. I forget what it's called.
stewart_read: Rep attempts to answer a closer-tier product question (battery options) and states incorrect product information — Ion carries only the Tesla Powerwall per the codex; there is no second battery brand. The rep introduces product uncertainty ('I forget what it's called') rather than using the universal escape hatch to bridge cleanly. This is a double gap: scope creep AND stating incorrect product information. The customer, who has done research and is actively comparing, is now carrying a false impression into the closer appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been trying to signal breadth of options to compete with Sunrun's two-battery offer. But the codex is explicit: Ion does not carry a second battery brand. The attempt to match Sunrun's offer with a product Ion doesn't have is the wrong move regardless of intent.
coaching_implication: Two coaching points in one moment. First, the scope discipline point: battery specs are closer-tier content. The universal escape hatch ('That's exactly what our specialist is built to walk you through') handles this cleanly without product risk. Second, the product accuracy point: the rep needs to know Ion's battery catalog is Powerwall-only. In the 1-on-1, confirm whether the rep genuinely believes Ion carries a second battery brand — if so, this is a product knowledge gap that needs floor-wide correction, not just individual coaching.
